draft.introduction: Thomas Paine’s Common Sense (1776) is the pamphlet that catalyzed American independence, translating Enlightenment political philosophy into plain language accessible to ordinary colonists. It sold over 500,000 copies in a population of 2.5 million.

translation.text: Some writers have so confounded society with government as to leave little or no distinction between them; whereas they are not only different but have different origins. Society is produced by our wants and government by our wickedness. Society in every state is a blessing; government, even in its best state, is but a necessary evil. The cause of America is in a great measure the cause of all mankind.

translator_notes.note: Paine’s distinction between society (natural, beneficial) and government (artificial, necessary evil) draws on Locke and Rousseau but with a rhetorical directness that neither achieved. His ability to make philosophical arguments in the language of the common person made Common Sense the most effective political pamphlet in history.
